title: Quantum magic of strongly correlated fermions $-$ the Hubbard dimer
authors: Edoardo Zavatti, Gabriele Bellomia, Massimo Capone
abstract: We study the non-stabilizerness (quantum magic) content of the Hubbard dimer, an analytically solvable, yet completely non-trivial, model of strongly correlated fermions. We can access zero- and finite-temperature properties as well as the time evolution in a quantum quench protocol. We evaluate local and nonlocal non-stabilizerness using both the robustness of magic and the stabilizer Renyi entropy, demonstrating how the latter often fails in detecting the mixed stabilizer states that are typically found in this kind of systems. Finally, we compare the non-stabilizerness with other genuine resources of quantum-state complexity, i.e., the fermionic non-Gaussianity and the superselected two-site entanglement. Our findings corroborate the notion of non-stabilizerness as a fundamentally different quantum resource, able to give profound insights that are missed by more traditional information-theoretic quantities.
summary: This paper investigates the 'quantum magic' of the Hubbard dimer, demonstrating that non-stabilizerness is a fundamental resource distinct from entanglement and non-Gaussianity. It shows that this magic peaks during electron localization and can be destroyed by temperature or dephasing. The work also highlights the unreliability of certain entropy-based measures for mixed fermionic states.
main_problem: The study aims to quantify 'quantum magic' (non-stabilizerness) in strongly correlated fermionic systems and determine if it serves as a distinct quantum resource compared to entanglement and non-Gaussianity.
main_result: Non-stabilizerness is identified as a unique resource that peaks during the transition to electron localization and can vanish due to thermal or dynamical processes, providing insights missed by traditional measures.
method: The authors use exact analytical solutions for the Hubbard dimer to evaluate various complexity metrics, including robustness of magic, stabilizer Renyi entropy, and fermionic non-Gaussianity.
model_or_system: The system is the Hubbard dimer, a two-site Hubbard model at half-filling characterized by a hopping amplitude (t) and local Coulomb repulsion (U).
key_observables: Non-stabilizerness (robustness of magic and stabilizer Renyi entropy), fermionic non-Gaussianity, inter-site entanglement under superselection rules, and double occupancy.
important_parameters: Interaction strength ratio (U/t), temperature (T), and dephasing rate (Gamma) during quantum quenches.
main_assumptions: The study is limited to the two-site Hubbard model, which is a simplified version of more complex many-body systems.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a geometric interpretation of the robustness of magic; Figure 2 compares non-stabilizerness, entanglement, and non-Gaussianity; Figure 7 illustrates how magic vanishes in certain density matrix mixtures.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of magic, presents exact solutions for the Hubbard dimer, analyzes zero- and finite-temperature properties, explores non-equilibrium quench dynamics with decoherence, and compares magic to other complexity measures.
why_interesting: It provides a deep look into how quantum complexity resources behave under thermalization and decoherence, which is highly relevant to the study of open quantum systems and many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Geometric interpretation of the robustness of magic. The hexagon represents the stabilizer polytope STABN for N = 1, i.e. all possible convex combinations of single qubit stabilizer states. Its vertices correspond to pure stabilizer states. The highlighted points correspond to the two (mixed) stabilizer states σ± such that the decomposition ρ = (1 + s)σ+ −sσ−is optimal. This is analogous to the formulation given in Eq. (1).

FIG. 2: Non-stabilizerness measures (panel a), inter- site entanglement under parity (P-SSR) and charge (N- SSR) super-selection rules (panel b), non-Gaussianity per site (panel c) and double occupancy (panel d) as functions of U/t for the pure ground state of the Hubbard dimer at zero temperature. Non-stabilizerness, entanglement and non- Gaussianity are expressed in bits (units of log(2)).

FIG. 3: Top panel: Local non-stabilizerness as a function of U/t, measured for the one-site reduced density matrix of the Hubbard dimer. While the system is clearly devoid of local magic, this is correctly captured only by LR, while the 2-SRE is everywhere non-zero. Bottom panel: local non-Gaussianity. Unlike magic and entanglement, non-Gaussianity is zero only at U = 0, highlighting the different nature of these quantities.

FIG. 4: Log-free robustness of magic for the Hubbard dimer at finite temperature. In (a) we display the U vs T phase diagram, showing that non-stabilizerness disappears in the region of large temperature and small Coulomb repulsion. The dashed white line represents the line of critical temperature Tc(U/t) where the magic drops to zero, while the dashed azure line marks the maximum of non-stabilizerness at each temperature. We further display the evolution of LR(ρ) and of the double occupancy ⟨d⟩as functions of temperature for fixed Coulomb repulsion in (b) and (c), while in (d) and (e) as functions of U/t in log-scale.

FIG. 5: Dynamics of non-stabilizerness after a quench at t = 0. Panels (a) and (e) display the evolution of LR and the 2-SRE, (b) and (f) of the inter-site entanglement under SSRs, (c) and (g) of the non-Gaussianity, and finally (d) and (h) of the double occupancy, after a quench from U = 100 to U = 5. When dephasing is absent (Γ = 0), non-stabilizerness oscillates periodically, while in presence of dephasing (Γ > 0) it saturates to a constant value. Panels (i) and (j) show the evolution of LR and of the 2-SRE respectively, for different values of the final value of the quench Uf.
